data.title: Tokens: Everything, Everywhere, All At Once?
data.description:
By Phil Mackintosh, Nasdaq Chief Economist

As the world talks more and more about “modernizing” existing financial market infrastructure, Nasdaq's new white paper estimates that 52% of institutions expect to be using tokenized collateral by the end of 2026.

It seems that everything can be a token, and they can be everywhere, all at once.

But the more you look, the less things look the same.

Tokens can be many things

One thing is clear: Tokens can be many different things.

Tokens range from non-fungible tokens (NFTs) like “Bored Ape” to cryptocurrency, which itself ranges from Dogecoin to bitcoin to stablecoins, and more recently, company stocks. That’s something investors (and companies that might see their stock trading in token form) probably don’t yet appreciate.

SEC announced a token taxonomy

In November 2025, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) Chair Paul Atkins gave a speech that laid out his vision of a token “taxonomy” to address the fact that everything was being called a token.

Given the SEC regulates “securities,” the taxonomy started based on the premise that “not all tokens are securities.” For instance, the SEC issued a statement that a “meme coin,” a type of crypto asset inspired by internet memes, characters, current events or trends, is not viewed as a security, since it does not involve the offer and sale of securities under the federal securities laws. Rather, this taxonomy is centered on the time-tested “Howey Test,” where to qualify as a security, a contract must satisfy four requirements:

  1. Investment of money: There must be an initial outlay of capital or other consideration of value.
  2. Common enterprise: The investor's money is pooled with that of other investors in a common venture, creating interdependence among participants.
  3. Expectation of profits: Investors must expect to earn returns on their investment, whether through capital appreciation or income distribution.
  4. Profits derived from the efforts of others: The profits must result primarily from the work and management of a third party (the promoter or operator), rather than from the investor's own efforts.

In that setting, NFTs or cryptocurrencies don’t qualify as securities (as there are no “efforts of others,” like a company’s employees). In fact, Chair Atkins divided the digital world into four categories, only one of which are securities:

Table 1: Token Taxonomy

Valuing assets the old-fashioned way

For those of us in the markets:

  • We know that not all securities need to pay dividends, the expectation of a capital gain is also “profit.”
  • But we are also used to being able to calculate an underlying “fair value,” even if it represents some future cash flows with inexact discounting valuation math.

Not all cryptos have assets backing them

Cryptocurrency has been held out as a potential medium of exchange - just like money. But crypto does not gain from the “efforts of others.” So, it’s not a security.

However, a subset of “cryptocurrency” is backed by actual money (aka stablecoin - like USDC). How these cryptocurrencies work in the U.S. is covered in the U.S. GENIUS act.

As we see in the chart below, stablecoins are just a fraction of total market cap of all crypto, even after the recent sell-off in many cryptocurrencies.

Chart 1: Cryptocurrency market cap over time

The majority of other coins may someday have utility - potentially in settlement and trade. But they only have value because markets say it does.

Bitcoin is somewhat unique because of the relatively expensive cost of mining and limits on the supply of tokens, which in theory supports scarcity and value.

Security tokens aren’t that different from traditional ETFs, ADRs, futures or swaps

Turning to the tokens that represent public companies (securities), as the market evolved, we see that they can take multiple forms.

A “native token” = A stock in digital form

Right now, when an investor owns Apple (AAPL) or Amazon (AMZN) in the U.S., it is recorded in a database owned by DTCC. That’s “digital” but not on Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT).

The owners of stocks have rights to vote and receive dividends.

It is possible a company stock could be represented on a blockchain database instead (or Nasdaq as well*). In that case, a buyer of the token should also have rights to vote and receive dividends. Although the settlement of the token can be “atomic” and the custody might be in your personal wallet.

Chart 2: An issuer-backed token represents a share in the company

*A DTCC-sponsored token would, in principle, work the same way - even if the token needed to be converted back into a private (DTCC) ledger - as it would represent a share of the actual company. However, it’s important to note that DTCC tokens are not native tokens; they are entitlements to the shares held in DTCC’s custody, meaning the token itself is not the actual share.

An Asset-Backed Token - Similar to an ETF or ADR

A fund or bank could accept investor inflows, buy underlying stock and issue tokens over those holdings.

These “tokens” would actually work very much like ADRs and ETFs work these days.

Buyers and sellers would know the “fair” value for the token - being the value of the underlying company(ies).

Chart 3: An asset-backed token looks more like an ETF or ADR today

For these tokens, the costs and ability to arbitrage would be important to ensure investors' prices are comparable to buying the stock directly.

Importantly, even if the underlying can be “created/redeemed” frictionlessly, there will be a difference in fair value thanks to atomic settlement. That’s because a buy of token would need to pay for their trade immediately, while the buyer of the stock gets to earn interest on their money for an extra day (until T+1 settlement).

Things get more complicated and expensive if different tokens on the same stock are not fungible, or transfer costs are high. In those instances, market makers might need to hold (and finance) a long position in one token, and a short position in another for an indefinite period of time.

At the extreme, if there is also no way to convert the token into the underlying asset (create or redeem), these tokens may trade more like closed-end-funds - with persistent premiums (or discounts).

Additionally, in third-party backed vehicles (like special purpose vehicles or SPVs), investors are exposed to the credit risk of the SPV, since the value of the token depends on the token issuer’s (SPV’s) ability to honor redemptions or maintain the backing assets. The use of leverage in these structures can further amplify both gains and losses, increasing the potential for volatility and systemic risk if the issuer faces financial difficulties or market conditions deteriorate.

An Unbacked Token - Similar to a Future or Swap

One of the most liquid financial instruments in the world today holds no underlying exposure - and that’s futures.

Instead of holding the underlying company or asset, financial futures have “open interest” - an equal number of buyers and sellers wanting economic exposure to an asset.

Chart 4: Tokens without any asset backing work more like futures or swaps do today

The economics of the underlying are retained by futures expiry. At that time, the profit on each position is determined based on the underlying asset and cash (or the underlying security) is exchanged between buyers and sellers.

One key utility of futures, including perpetual futures, is that they allow market participants to efficiently hedge risk or speculate on price movements without owning the underlying asset. Perpetual futures provide ongoing exposure without a set expiration date, and margin requirements are essential as they help manage risk and ensure participants maintain sufficient collateral to cover potential losses.

Consequently, continuous public prices for the underlying asset are critical to ensure the contract roughly tracks the price of the underlying asset.

However, we have already seen “token” derivatives issued over private companies - like the SpaceX token. Interestingly, in this instance, the company itself said it had nothing to do with the token.

Valuing private companies creates complications. Tokens on illiquid assets will have less frequent “margin calls,” which may expose investors to single-party credit risks - more like how swaps sometimes work.

Everything, everywhere, but also very different things, all at once

It seems, for now at least, that everything can be tokenized.

And regulators around the world have started to clear the way for investments like stocks, bonds and funds to trade in a tokenized form.

Call it DeFi, and disruption, but in many ways these structures aren’t that different to things we have been trading for decades. It's just that now we call them a lot of different things that help investors understand how they work.

The word “token” can actually mean many different kinds of exposures. That’s something that regulators (and investors) need to account for when making rules (and investment decisions).

Knowing how markets and arbitrage work, it’s likely to be important that investors know the difference.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dell: A Value Stock Multiple On An AI Growth Business
data.description:
I initiate my coverage on Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) with a Strong Buy rating, citing its strong AI backlog growth and a guidance for this fiscal year ($50B) that implies a near 2x YOY increase in AI server revenue.

Yes, the company is much more than just AI servers, and the non-AI products aren't growing as much (or at all, as I'm about to show in this piece). In fact, my outlook for the client solutions group is not particularly exciting.

That said, it's impossible to ignore how the company surprised the Street in each of the last 3 quarters, prompting several revenue upgrades.

Overall, the Street expects the current growth to normalize to just 1% in the April 2027 quarter. Call me crazy, but I think the company may be able to surprise the consensus, as the second half of the year has multiple products (mainly the PowerEdge XE9812) in the pipeline targeted for hyperscalers. Once again, I believe the Street is underestimating the traction that these products may have.

At 13.8x forward earnings, I think even the value guys may agree that the stock is cheap, at least from a valuation perspective.

AI Backlog And Strong Guidance

Let's look at the backlog first.

Dell reported $34.1B in AI orders in fiscal Q4 FY26 and exited the quarter with an AI backlog of $43B. The significant spike in AI orders during the quarter led management to project roughly $50B of AI server revenue in fiscal 2027, up about 103% YOY. For reference, fiscal Q1 AI server revenue was guided to roughly $13 billion.

That guidance and visibility prompted the Street to revise its revenue models once again.

Remember the chart above, as I will refer to it in the next section.

Why is that chart so important? Well, in my experience, price action always follows growth. As shown below, the share price tends to converge with the next 12-month sales line (in blue). As long as the blue line goes up, the price should eventually follow.

More on this topic later.

Moving on, during a recent Morgan Stanley conference, the CFO said the enterprise AI customer base grew from 3,300 to more than 4,000 in roughly 90 days. Here is the full excerpt, as it reveals a key data point for this quarter (in bold, emphasis added):

From an enterprise perspective, we talked 90 days ago when we did Q3 earnings about having 3,300 enterprise AI customers in our installed base. Just last Thursday, we updated that 90 days later to be 4,000 plus. That sequential jump, that was the strongest enterprise quarter we've had. And now you look at our pipeline, all pipeline verticals grew. The percentage increase that was the fastest was that enterprise cohort as well.

Looking at the enterprise segment, the March 2026 roadmap showed the PowerEdge R9822 and M9822 rackmount enterprise servers, which will integrate Nvidia's Vera CPU, available in September.

That should be a nice catalyst in the second half of the year, as I think many companies may be holding back on spending until Nvidia makes the Vera Rubin widely available.

Moving on to the traditional Client Solutions Group, fiscal Q4 showed revenue of $13.5 billion, up 14% YOY, with commercial revenue of $11.6 billion, up 16% YOY.

Let me put that into perspective. Q4 FY26 commercial revenue growth was the sixth straight quarter of growth, and demand increased for the eighth straight quarter.

A big tailwind to this segment last year was Microsoft (MSFT) ending support for Windows 10 on October 14. Since then, it seems that there has been a second catalyst.

Gartner reported last week that Dell’s worldwide PC shipments increased to 10.337 million units in calendar Q1 2026 from 9.608 million a year earlier and that its market share rose to 16.5% from 15.9%.

Great news, right? Maybe not.

According to the report, it appears that Q1 shipment growth was artificially inflated by inventory builds ahead of expected Q2 price increases tied to rising memory costs. This brings me to the next section.

Risks

Let’s focus first on the main segment the market is paying attention to.

Not everything is rainbows and sunshine in Dell's infrastructure segment. For example, back in Q3 FY26, the non-AI infrastructure business (i.e., storage) was down by 1%:

In the last quarter, storage revenue was $4.8 billion (accounting for 24% of the revenue of the ISG segment), up by only 2% YOY.

In other words, about a quarter of the segment the market is closely watching is barely growing its top line. If we look at a full-year basis, FY26 storage revenue was $16.6 billion, up just 1%, far behind the AI-optimized server growth of 166%.

Moving on to the traditional segment of the company, the revenue of the Client Solutions Group segment was up by just 5% in FY 2026. That segment accounted for 45% of the total sales during the last fiscal year.

So, again, we have the same problem. A good part of the top line is not growing as it should (i.e., double digits, at least).

On top of that, the outlook for the CSG segment is anything but favorable. Back in January, IDC expected the PC market to shrink at least 4.9% in 2026 after growing by 8.1% last year.

In fact, that's the moderate scenario. The draconian scenario (in orange) is projecting a near double-digit decline in PC shipments.

The culprit? The hike in memory prices is because high bandwidth memory, or HBM, is the bottleneck in AI chip manufacturing. For reference, we finally have the forecast for Q2 this year:

I say "finally" because I own a few memory names, like Micron (MU), Sandisk (SNDK), and Seagate (STX). These names are bullish as long as the memory supercycle is not showing any signs of deterioration. As a side note, I don't expect that to happen until early 2027 (at the earliest).

Therefore, I am not surprised to see the Street's revenue forecast for the company in the next few quarters:

To be honest, the only reason I am not panic-selling my Dell position, given the growth deterioration forecasted above, is the fact that the company has been surprising the Street, particularly in Q4 FY26, prompting new upward revisions.

In other words, analysts have been underestimating the growth of this company.

Last, but not least, margins.

The AI server space is not a high-margin business due to costs and intense competition from SMCI and HPE. The chart below, showing the company's gross margins in the last few years, is not particularly exciting.

As I noted before, the hike in memory costs may be one of the main drivers, as I believe it's unlikely the company is passing 100% of the memory hike prices to consumers. Therefore, the deterioration in gross and operating margins.

Valuation

Dirt cheap is how I'd categorize a 13.8x forward P/E multiple for a stock with direct exposure to the AI trade. That said, Dell is not the cheapest exposure to the servers, storage, and enterprise infrastructure themes. That title goes to Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), which has a forward P/E multiple of only 10x.

That said, the exposure of Dell is significantly higher than for HPE. For example, HPE's latest headline numbers are $1.1B in AI-related revenue and >$5B backlog.

In comparison, Dell reported in the last quarter $9.0B in AI server revenue and a $43B AI backlog. Dell has a more pure exposure to AI than HPE, which is one of the reasons I own it despite it being more expensive. In fact, that's why I believe Dell has outperformed HPE in terms of returns in the last year.

Looking Ahead

What caught my eye with Dell was its speed of upward revenue revisions.

Back in August last year, Dell raised FY 2026 AI server expectations to about $20 billion, then to about $25 billion in November 2025, and then guided to roughly $50 billion of AI server revenue in FY 2027.

In each of the last 3 earnings releases, the Street has revised its revenue models upwards. This suggests that the market is underestimating the growth potential of this giant.

Therefore, even though the current expectations for growth suggest a sharp deceleration from 52% revenue growth in the April 2026 quarter to just 1% growth in the April 2027 quarter, I believe this forecast is overly pessimistic.

Given what's going on with Super Micro (SMCI), I wouldn't be surprised to see a hike in AI backlog this quarter, as some customers may have reconsidered their rack server provider.

Overall, despite the CSG segment likely underperforming this year, I rate Dell as a Strong Buy, and I own the stock in my portfolio.

data.title: Bloom Energy: Oracle Reinforces AI Power Surge
data.description:
Bloom Energy Corporation (BE) powered to new highs on excitement over a large expanded deal with Oracle (ORCL). The fuel cell company has become a leading force in providing data center power where utility supply doesn't exist. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on the stock, though the stock gains likely need to consolidate before the next rally.

Big Oracle Deal

Bloom Energy announced a deal with Oracle, procuring up to 2.8 GW of fuel cell systems. The agreement outlines an initial 1.2 GW of capacity, with deployment underway and continuing into 2027.

The big story with Bloom Energy over the last six months or so was the internal claim of having the capacity for substantial growth, along with the ability to supply data center power quickly. Back on the Q3'25 earnings call, the CEO claimed Bloom Energy was expanding the capacity for 2 GW in annual production by the end of 2026, amounting to 4x the 2025 revenues of $1.9 billion, or the equivalent of $7.6 billion in revenues.

The company claims to have delivered a fully operational full-cell system to Oracle in just 55 days. A prime example of why Bloom Energy would receive an expanded order.

Due to past deals, Bloom Energy had issued warrants to Oracle. The hyperscaler has the right to purchase 3.53 million warrants at an exercise price of $113.28, now nearly $100 in the money.

Worth noting, Bloom Energy has previously listed a total backlog of $20 billion, with ~$6 billion tied to Products. For 2025, ~75% of revenues were tied to Products with Service revenues only amounting to $228 million.

Big 2026 In The Cards

The company will report Q1'26 results on April 28. The big focus is how much Bloom Energy can further ramp up production, with Oracle claiming upwards of 60% of expanded capacity targets, though the order appears spread out over two years.

Bloom Energy guided to 2026 revenues of ~$3.2 billion for over 50% growth. The company is already very profitable, unlike other modern energy sources, with gross margins of 32% and operating income targets at $450 million for 2026.

The big question here is exactly where this Oracle order fits into the prior guidance. Oracle was already a key customer, so management had to have some indication of this order when reporting Q4'25 results in early February.

The big Oracle order doesn't necessarily boost the 2026 guidance, but it clearly provides support for 2027 revenues jumping another 50% to $5.1 billion. The current 2028 revenue target of $7.2 billion is far more reasonable considering Oracle has another 1.6 GW of additional orders after 2027.

Note, the 2028 revenue guidance isn't even above the current capacity target, and 2 GW of power isn't substantial in a time when 10 GW campuses are regularly discussed in the market now. Bloom Energy would seem to have the potential to supply far higher power levels with the big pushback in data centers hooking up to the power lines.

A city in Missouri just fired half the city council after approving a data center planning to spend up to $6 billion in contraction and property additions. A part of the issue is concerns over electric rates and water use.

The stock has seen the market cap soar to $60 billion on the excitement over the expanded Oracle deal. Bloom Energy already trades at 8.5x the 2028 revenue targets, and the market will shift to start valuing the energy company by earnings.

Bloom Energy trades at 45x 2028 EPS targets of $4.86. Even if the company pulls forward results by a year due to huge data center power demands, the stock still trades at nearly 30x 2029 EPS targets of $7.43 on revenue of $9.4 billion.

Investors have to be cognizant of the big stock run, nearly triple the lows just back in December. Bloom Energy is slightly expensive based on results a couple of years out, and no guarantee exists that AI data center demand will end up meeting lofty goals.

The stock traded at less than $20 a year ago, so Bloom Energy is clearly very reliant on the AI data center buildout and need for external power supplies in order to support the stock price. Bloom would lose a lot of steam on a solution to the power shortage via either utilities stepping up to connect data centers or an alternative power solution able to supply energy more efficiently than their fuel-cell systems.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Bloom Energy has the potential to dramatically increase financial targets due to soaring AI data center demand from the likes of Oracle. The stock trades slightly expensive based on 2028 estimates, but the opportunity exists for the company to easily surpass these numbers.

Investors should let Bloom Energy consolidate here and possibly retest support around $180 before adding more shares.

data.title: BATT: Powering Tomorrow, Overextended Today
data.description:
Amplify Lithium & Battery Technology ETF (BATT) can be considered an all-encompassing play on the energy transition globally, offering a complete view of battery manufacturing in its entirety from "mine to motor." Compared to its peers, the BATT ETF has an exceptionally diverse portfolio that invests heavily in leading miners such as BHP Group Limited (BHP) and Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (FCX) as well as prominent battery manufacturers like Contemporary Amperex Technology Co., Limited (CYATY) (more known as CATL) and BYD Company Limited (BYDDF). BATT was created in June 2018, aiming to replicate the performance of the EQM Lithium & Battery Technology Index. The BATT ETF has 53 constituents in its portfolio, with some of its top picks also including Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), BYD Company Limited, and Freeport-McMoRan at positions ranging from 8% to 5%. The total percentage allocated to the top ten picks in this ETF stands at around 45%. Additionally, the ETF has an expense ratio of 0.59%, and its assets under management total about $123 million.

The Fund

Regarding the fund itself, the expense ratio is 59 basis points, and it is assigned a Seeking Alpha C Expense Grade. Liquidity is average but still satisfactory for a retail investor, with an average daily dollar volume on a 3-month basis around $1 million and an AUM of $120 million, earning it a Seeking Alpha C Grade. In terms of the Dividend Grade, it's below average, ranking it with a D+ Dividend Grade, with a below-median dividend yield of 1.6% and a negative dividend growth rate of 19.23% in the last three years.

The fund's sector structure, as expected, is concentrated in the Basic Materials sector, with 54% of the fund's assets. Consumer cyclical and Industrials present the second and third largest sector exposure. The country allocation is diversified, although investors should be wary of the relatively high exposure to developing markets and China specifically, given elevated geopolitical risks.

Performance-wise, BATT has exhibited great returns, especially short term, with a 116% total 1-year return, scoring an excellent Momentum grade. The 5-year total return is average, around 21%.

Lithium Supply Deficit, Structural Tailwinds And AI Demand

The catalyst environment for BATT is currently experiencing a "perfect storm" of forces coming together that will play out over the next few years. In the short term, the main driver is the rapid tightening of the market balance on lithium. Following years of oversupply and falling prices leading up to 2024, a move to a supply deficit is predicted by 2026 to be as high as 80 thousand tonnes. This trend is being amplified by a huge inventory cycle coupled with a growing demand from Energy Storage Systems (ESS), which have risen in market share to 31% of all lithium consumption, up from only 9% previously. Additionally, geopolitical turbulence in the Middle East region has driven oil prices up, forcing the "cost-push" factor onto individuals and firms to switch to electric products. As an added advantage, BATT’s exposure to copper (at around 20%) also creates a short-term upside due to the huge quantities required in grid upgrades and AI data centers. I wrote my views regarding copper in my article about the Global X Copper Miners ETF (COPX); if you are interested, go and give it a look. I express my opinion in broader detail; I will just say that I am bullish on the metal, and BATT’s exposure to copper is certainly a strong point in my opinion. These are all positive catalysts that I expect will present support for the fund in the period ahead, but we must pair them together with the risk environment for the fund to get a clear picture of the near-term setup.

The structural growth narrative forming the foundation for the BATT long-term investment thesis remains compelling. In terms of the overall global lithium-ion battery market, it is currently forecast to grow from its current level of $194.7 billion in 2025 to reach $426.4 billion in 2033. While growth from EV adoption and other related factors is expected to help drive growth in the market, the structural growth opportunity created through the transition to stationary battery storage is likely to become one of the most overlooked opportunities of all. The worldwide lithium-ion battery market consumed 1.59 TWh in 2025, growing 29% YoY, while battery systems used in energy storage systems grew 51% compared to 26% for EVs.

The combination of data center power needs driven by artificial intelligence and the need for integrating renewable energy results in a completely new class of infrastructure customers, namely the hyperscalers that have both the means and the imperative to invest in large-scale battery storage systems quickly. The annual U.S. installations of BESS are expected to be higher than 110 GWh by 2030, with cumulative capacity reaching almost 500 GWh. From a long-term perspective, the future surely looks bright, with demand seemingly strong for now.

Valuation

Valuation is still reasonable with a P/E of 19.64x, trading at a discount to the Nasdaq's 22.8x. The P/B is significantly lower, with forecasted higher earnings growth and lower price-to-cash flow compared to the Nasdaq valuation.

Risks

Despite the positive growth story, there remain certain risks. The first risk is associated with the heavy concentration of the battery supply chain in China, with companies predicted to have 50% of global lithium mining operations controlled by them by 2027. We are witnesses of the geopolitical turmoil and weakening China-U.S. relationships, from the tariff policy that upset markets last year to China tightening crucial rare earth exports. This, in my opinion, expresses willingness on both sides to endorse strategic hardline rules and restrictions, which does not look favorable on the fund. The second risk comes from the end of the EV subsidy programs, which means that all the players in the industry now rely on price-based competition. This is likely to reduce the profitability of EV manufacturing, which could affect the manufacturers included in the portfolio, such as Tesla and BYD. There is also a risk of technology disruption, even though the current battery technology relies heavily on lithium. If a more viable alternative is commercially developed faster than expected, it would certainly impact fund constituents.

Conclusion

BATT presents a high-conviction investment in the structural growth of the battery ecosystem. While long-term secular trends arising from the energy transition and AI energy demand, as well as the expected lithium supply deficit, present upside potential for the fund, the current geopolitical environment paired with tariff uncertainty present real risks. That said, the near-term risk-reward setup looks unattractive after the fund's hot run, positioning me to issue a HOLD rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Golub Capital BDC: An Underrated Opportunity In The BDC Sector
data.description:
In this anаlysis, we examine the Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) and its key financial mеtrics - assеts, dеbt, crеdit rаting, and histоrical pеrformаncе - tо assеss whеther GBDC's cоmmon stоck rеpresents an аttractive invеstment oppоrtunity cоmpared to altеrnatives in thе bоnd mаrket. If you would like to look at our last article for the company, you can do so here.

Assets, Portfolio, and Debt

At the end of 2025, Golub Capital BDC, Inc. (GBDC) had close to $8.9 billion in total assets and $4.98 billion in total debt. The investments at fair value represent close to 97% of the total assets, and the non-accrual loans represent 0.8% of total investments at fair value. The current market capitalization is close to $3.45 billion.

The biggest part of its portfolio is in the Software industry (27%), followed by Healthcare Providers & Services (7%). The largest part of the company's investment portfolio is in First lien - close to 92%, followed by 7% in "Equity," and 1% in "Junior Debt."

In the next picture shows the portfolio highlights of GBDC:

Credit Rating

GBDC is rated as "investment-grade." Moody's gives it a "Baa2" credit rating, S&P - a "BBB-," and Fitch - a "BBB."

And below you can see the debt capital structure of GBDC as of the last quarter:

Historical Performance

The five-year average annual total return on NAV is close to 10.25%, which is higher than the peer group's 7.18%. And the 3-year average annual total return on NAV is around 11.84%, 4.9 points higher than the peer group. The 10-year average annual total return again is higher than the peer group's - 8.92%, while the peer group's is close to 7.19%.

Historical Premium/Discount

The next chart is the 10-year historical premiums/discounts. We see GBDC currently at one of its lowest points. The last time the discount was this great was around the COVID pandemic.

At the time of writing, the common stock is trading at a discount close to 12%, which is far from its average discount for the last five years, close to 2.8%:

Expenses

The operating expenses of GBDC are close to 20% of the total investment income, which is close to $857 million at the end of 2025. With assets of around $8.9 billion, the gross assets yield is close to 9.7%. The non-leverage expenses are close to 2%.

Comparisons

Below is shown the BDC OTC universe:

The highest yield to maturity is close to 7.4%, but the average yield to maturity of the group is around 6%.

The average asset yield of GBDC is around 9.7%, and the average loss of yield is close to 0.67%, and for the last three years, the average loss/total income is around 7%. When we include all of these, the expected ROE for the common stock is around 8.34%, but because the common stock is at a nearly 12% discount, the discount-adjusted ROE increases to around 9.48%. These values ​​are derived from historical data over the past three years. To make the proper comparison with the bonds, we add leverage to the average OTC bond and consider a hypothetical capital structure with 200 total assets and 100 net assets. The expected leveraged-bond ROE is close to 8.50%. You can see the full comparison tables below:

From this comparison, we see that the common stock is offering a higher return. The stock's discount-adjusted ROE jumps to around 9.5% - a percentage point more than the bond alternative. It all depends on each investor's risk tolerance. We offer to your attention two valid investment options; you can pick either or none.

And here you can see GBDS's OTC bond:

Currently, the bond has close to a 5.35% yield to maturity. Let's do the same comparison with the common stock:

The leveraged GBDC's OTC bond has close to 7.2% ROE, which is ~2.3 points lower than the adjusted ROE of the common stock - if we have to choose between GBDC's financial instruments, it would definitely be the common stock.

Risk

Dеspite GBDC's attrаctive profile, investors shоuld be awаre of sevеral key risks. Rising interеst rates could impаct the quаlity of the pоrtfolio and increase the nоn-accrual loan ratio, which currently stands at 0.8%. As a levеraged compаny, GBDC is sеnsitive to chаnges in the cоst of funding, which cоuld rеduce nеt intеrest margin. Lаst but nоt least, thе concentration in the software sector carries sector risk in the evеnt of a dоwnturn in the technоlogy industry.

Conclusion

GBDC has a solid portfоlio, an investment-grade crеdit rating, and has historically outperformed its peers' total returns. The current discоunt of around 12% to NAV creаtes an аdditional oppоrtunity for futurе rеturns if thе price incrеases. Alsо, with an аdjusted ROE of around 9.5% vs. close to 8.5% for the sector's average OTC bond and vs. close to 7.2% of its own OTC bond, GBDC common stock appеars to be an attractive choice for investors willing to take on additional risk. As we said already, it all depends on each investor's risk tolerance.

data.title: Allbirds: From Sneakers To Servers; Why The Transformation To AI Warrants A Sell
data.description:
Allbirds, Inc. (BIRD) skyrocketed from ~$2.50 to $17 per share on the day following news of a pivot from shoes to AI compute infrastructure. The stock then corrected about ~30% from all-time highs and currently trades in the ~$11-12 range.

At the time of writing, Allbirds only capitalizes at about ~$100 million and is classified as a microcap. At the time of its IPO in 2021, the company was capitalized at ~$4 billion. Today, I am going to discuss whether an investment case can be made.

Allbirds has no moat in AI

At the cost of stating the obvious, I do not think that Allbirds has any moat or edge in the AI space:

  • Their Direct-To-Consumer eCommerce fashion business provides no specific expertise in the AI field or in building data centers.

  • Their existing contacts and distributor agreements in the wholesale fashion space provide no specific advantage either.

  • Their owned store count has no relevance to AI, nor does management have previous AI experience.

This is key to my valuation model and SELL rating. Because effectively, I believe investing in BIRD after its recent run is the equivalent of investing in a new company that announced they want to borrow ~$50 million to buy AI chips and rent them out.

A weak financial position means the AI pivot starts from scratch

The financial situation of BIRD can be described, in my view, as critical. Key financial metrics (from Seeking Alpha) are reported in the table above. At the time of writing, the company’s cash position is negative after accounting for debt, and BIRD is also FCF negative and unprofitable.

Metric

Dec 2025

Dec 2024

Dec 2023

Dec 2022

Cash & ST Investments

$26.7M

$66.7M

$130.0M

$167.1M

Total Assets

$109.4M

$188.9M

$312.7M

$462.4M

Total Debt

$39.6M

$53.7M

$93.9M

$105.8M

Net Debt/(Cash)

$13.0M

-$13.1M

-$36.1M

-$61.3M

Operating Cash Flow

-$55.1M

-$63.9M

-$30.2M

-$90.6M

Net Income

-$77.3M

-$93.3M

-$152.5M

-$101.4M

Employees

362

542

927

1,000

What this means is that the company has no available financial resources to deploy for their AI pivot. It is no surprise, then, that management announced they plan to sell the entire legacy shoes business for $39 million to American Exchange Group.

BIRD then plans to raise $50 million for their AI pivot in the form of convertible notes from an unnamed institutional investor.

Dilution risk in itself warrants a SELL

Raising money in the form of convertible notes represents a significant dilution risk for shareholders. Allbirds currently has 8.7 million shares outstanding. $50 million at a $2.99 conversion price would represent an extra 16.72 million shares that could be sold into the market (note: the $2.99 figure is just my guess based on where the company was trading before the announcement, as the conversion price has not been disclosed yet).

Even if the conversion price ends up being closer to BIRD’s current share price, let’s say at $15, this would represent a ~28% dilution. Note that I find such a high conversion price as unlikely, given the company was nowhere near that price before signing the deal with the unnamed institutional investor.

This dilution risk in itself warrants a Sell rating from the share price at the time of writing, in my opinion.

To be fair, conversion to equity requires separate stockholder approval at the Special Meeting on May 18th. However, given how beneficial this pivot is to the long-term survival of the company, I would expect the resolution to go through.

Will NewBird AI be debt-free?

Another big question mark and potential risk to BIRD’s AI pivot concerns whether or not the new emerging company will be debt-free.

The 8-K explicitly states that American Exchange Group will acquire "all of the intellectual property and certain other assets and liabilities." Critically, the 8-K also confirms Allbirds amended its ABL credit agreement to obtain lender consent to the sale. This means the ABL revolver ($17.4 million) is very likely repaid at closing from the $39 million gross proceeds, not passed to NewBird AI. The capital leases ($15.5 million) are store-related and almost certainly transfer with the shoe business to the acquirer. The net result is that NewBird AI should emerge essentially debt-free. However, this is not confirmed yet, and it will be subject to confirmation in the proxy filing due April 24.

The AI bull case

Assuming “NewBird AI” will emerge debt-free, the new company will have, in my estimates, approximately ~$93 million available for their AI pivot (see table below).

Source

Amount

Existing cash (Dec 2025)

$26.7M

Asset sale gross proceeds

$39.0M

ABL revolver repayment

-$17.4M

Wind-down/legal/severance costs (est.)

-$5.0M

Net cash from shoe sale

$16.6M

$50M convertible facility

$50.0M

Total capital available

$93.3M

Remaining debt

~$0

I can assume the company would deploy this capital as follows:

  • ~$48 million in GPUs, resulting in ~1,500 H100 GPUs at $32k each

  • ~$10-12 million in Infrastructure (networking, power, cooling, etc.)

  • ~$10-12 million for Headcount (the company needs to hire specialists) and OpEx

  • ~$10 million as working capital contingency

  • ~$10 million as a buffer, possibly to cover a special dividend the company has announced but not quantified yet

This would be sufficient, in my view, to start their AI pivot.

NewBird AI could be worth between ~$70 million and ~$150 million

To understand what the resulting company could be worth, I drafted 3 scenarios (a bear, a base, and a bull case), with different assumptions (based on current market rates) regarding the price BIRD could sell their compute power at and gross margins.

These scenarios result in a company that could generate between $14 million and $29 million, with profitability only achievable in the bull case, as the table below shows.

Scenario

Gross Revenue

Gross Profit

GM%

EBITDA

EBITDA%

Bear ($1.50/hr, 70% util)

$13.8M

$6.3M

45.8%

-$1.7M

-12.2%

Base ($2.00/hr, 80% util)

$21.0M

$13.5M

64.0%

+$5.5M

Bull ($2.50/hr, 90% util)

At a rich 5x revenue valuation multiple, this would imply a $69–148 million valuation, as the table below shows.

Scenario

5x Revenue

10x EBITDA
