$139.2M

$208.8M

$278.4M

The company could, theoretically, be valued more if the market decided to have it trade at 20X its EBITDA in the bull case. I find this latter scenario extremely unlikely, however, since BIRD has no specific moat to justify a particularly rich valuation.

Allbirds is already priced for perfection

At the time of writing, BIRD already trades at a valuation of ~$100 million, roughly matching my base scenario. This accounts for the company renting out GPUs at $2/hour with an 80% utilization and achieving 64% gross margins.

Not only is this, in my view, a fairly optimistic scenario, but it doesn’t account for any dilution of existing shareholders. Considering that dilution has the potential in itself to more than triple the current share count, I cannot find a case for holding the stock, let alone buying it at current prices. I therefore recommend a SELL.

Risks to my thesis

Despite my bearish coverage, I have to mention that, barring shareholder dilution, the company trades at a relatively fair valuation relative to its potential. My own valuation model sees potential further upside, especially if the company manages to flawlessly execute their pivot and rapidly achieve profitability. Even if they don't, the company could return to its recent, post-pivot all-time highs just based on market sentiment. This would represent a fairly significant upside of ~50%.

In this context, BIRD’s valuation could theoretically stretch further, especially in the case of a continued AI-driven tech bull run. This is the main risk to my thesis.

What BIRD's pivot says about an "AI bubble"

News related to BIRD is not, in itself, of any specific relevance to whether or not we are in an AI bubble. We are, after all, talking about a microcap stock with no current operations in any field related to AI.

Nevertheless, I think that BIRD's AI pivot could prove useful in the future to gauge whether or not we are in an AI bubble and to what extent. Specifically, I think it will be interesting to assess whether the company will easily find demand for its (soon-to-come) computing power and at what prices.

If BIRD does manage to meet the metrics of my own bullish scenario, it may signal that demand for AI compute is so strong that even a new player with no previous experience in the field can make a profit by simply raising money and buying GPUs. That would, in my opinion, be a strong indicator that AI is nowhere near a bubble.

Conversely, if BIRD does not manage to get close to the bullish assumptions of my valuation model, it may indicate that demand is not strong enough for anyone to compete and that we may be in bubble territory.

It will also be interesting to observe the direction of the stock in the coming days and weeks. While at the moment its market cap simply matches a bullish (but realistic) scenario, if we were in a bubble, I think we could see hype taking over and valuations becoming very stretched.

Regarding valuations, readers that are interested in whether or not we are in an AI bubble may benefit from reading my 2026 market outlook, where I outline what an actual AI bubble may look like and what valuations we may see if we entered one.

Conclusion

Anyone that has enough credibility to raise $50-100 million can fundamentally copy Allbirds' AI “playbook,” in my view, which has no competitive advantage in AI, at least in my opinion.

The company currently trades at a valuation that already prices in a mildly bullish scenario (my base case), with BIRD successfully pivoting to AI and starting to generate ~$21 million in revenue. I think that the upside is therefore fairly limited. Considering the risks involved in a pivot, and especially shareholder dilution coming from the convertible notes, I think that a SELL case is warranted for BIRD.

This said, I also see no evidence within this specific news of an "AI bubble" of any sort. It will be interesting to watch whether the pivot is successful. If it is, it may indicate that AI demand is very strong and we are, in fact, not in an AI bubble.

data.title: Boeing: Oil Shock Hits Airlines, But Demand For Jets Won't Break
data.description:
Airlines are currently going through a tough patch as higher oil prices have increased costs and macro softening may soften demand. The reason, of course, is the situation in the Middle East. In a separate report, I discussed The Boeing Company’s (BA) airplane programs that have elevated exposure to the airlines in the Middle East. However, we note that in the longer term, the industry is resilient. What remains is that we need to carefully assess Boeing’s recovery path as the supply of new airplanes still falls short of demand. In this report, I discuss Boeing’s airplane orders and deliveries in March.

Boeing 737 MAX And Dreamliner Drive The Order Inflow

For this report, I'm using a tool developed by The Aerospace Forum to visualize airplane orders, deliveries, cancellations, and associated values.

In March, Boeing booked 33 orders compared to 21 orders booked in February. The order inflow consisted of 25 single-aisle airplanes and seven wide-body airplanes with a combined value of $2.8 billion:

  • A VIP customer ordered one Boeing 737 MAX.
  • An unidentified customer ordered 19 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer ordered five Boeing 737-800As, which is the military derivative of the Boeing 737.
  • An unidentified customer ordered eight Boeing 787-9s.

During the month, the following changes were made to the order book:

  • Cambodia Airways Co., Ltd. (CAKR) was identified as the customer for ten 737 MAX aircraft.
  • CDB Leasing was identified as the customer for one 737 MAX aircraft.
  • SMBC Aviation Capital took over a delivery slot for thirty-six 737 MAX aircraft from Air Lease Corporation and was identified as the customer for seven additional 737 MAX aircraft.
  • SMBC Aviation Capital took over a delivery slot for six 787-9 aircraft from Air Lease Corporation.
  • TUI Travel Aviation Finance Limited took over a delivery slot for one 737 MAX aircraft from BOC Aviation Limited (BCVVF).
  • Japan Airlines (JAPSY) selected GE engines for three 787-9 aircraft.
  • EVA Air selected GE engines for four 787-10 aircraft.
  • Deutsche Lufthansa AG (DLAKF) selected General Electric Company (GE) engines for two 787-9 aircraft.
  • Royal Jordanian selected GE engines for four 787-9 aircraft.
  • MSC Air Cargo Holding SA was identified as the customer for one 777F aircraft.
  • Bank of Communications Leasing was identified as the customer for one 737 MAX aircraft.
  • Air Europa cancelled one 737 MAX aircraft.
  • Enter Air Sp. z o.o. cancelled one 737 MAX aircraft.
  • Vietnam Airlines’ 10 Boeing 737 MAX orders were moved to the unidentified category.

The order inflow in March was not huge, with only 33 orders from unidentified customers. After subtracting two cancellations, Boeing was left with 31 orders valued at $2.7 billion. During the month, we saw various operators selecting the GE turbofans for their Boeing 787s, aligning with my view that GE Aerospace has the upper hand against Rolls-Royce Holdings plc (RYCEF) on the Boeing 787 engine selection. We also saw SMBC Aviation taking over slots from Air Lease Corporation, which aligns with the acquisition of Air Lease Corporation being completed.

In April 2025, Boeing had a substantially higher order inflow driven by big orders from Korean Air and BOC Aviation Limited (BCVVF). Boeing’s net orders reached 163 orders valued at $20 billion. Year-on-year, we see a substantial reduction in airplane order volumes and values as the mix leaned more towards wide-body airplanes last year. Year-to-date, Boeing has booked 149 net orders valued at $13.6 billion compared to 204 net orders valued at $22.1 billion a year ago. So, we do see that net orders were substantially lower this year. However, at this point we cannot conclude that the war is pressuring order inflow, as major orders such as the one from Korean Air last year do not occur each year.

ASC 606 adjustments increased by nine units for the Boeing 737 program. ASC 606 adjustments might be a vague term. Therefore, I have provided a description from prior analyses about what they are:

ASC 606 adjustments are corrections to the backlog for orders where a purchase contract exists, but additional criteria are not met. A decrease in ASC 606 adjustments can be achieved when the order, in fact, does get cancelled or the additional criteria are met.

Boeing currently has a total of 599 orders in the ASC 606 adjustment category, which covers 8.9% of the backlog, up from 8.8% in the prior month. It does not mean that 8.9% of the backlog is necessarily prone to cancellation, but for 8.9% of the backlog, additional criteria are not yet met. Occasionally, this leads to cancellations. During the month, the tally increased for the Boeing 737 MAX, likely driven by the Vietnam Airlines orders being moved into the category and one order that was already in the category being cancelled, driving a one-unit decrease in the tally.

Boeing Airplane Deliveries Show Little Growth

In March, Boeing delivered 46 airplanes consisting of 34 single-aisle airplanes and 12 wide-body aircraft valued at $3.6 billion:

  • Boeing delivered 34 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes, including one military derivative of the Boeing 737.
  • Boeing delivered two Boeing 767s, including one freighter and one base configuration for the KC-46A tanker.
  • Boeing delivered three Boeing 777 freighters.
  • Boeing delivered seven Boeing 787s consisting of six -9s and one -10.

Sequentially, Boeing’s delivery numbers fell from 51 to 46. Normally, airplane deliveries increase towards the end of the quarter. However, in March, Boeing 737 MAX deliveries declined from 43 units to 34 units as the jet maker had to rectify a wiring issue. It should not affect the company’s full-year performance but did affect the March deliveries and therefore the Q1 deliveries. This was partially offset by higher Boeing 777 and Boeing 787 deliveries, while Boeing 767 deliveries reduced by one unit. In the same month last year, Boeing delivered 41 airplanes valued at $2.9 billion. Driven by higher Boeing 787 deliveries and more military derivatives in the mix, the delivery value grew 24% on a 12% increase in delivery volumes.

Year-to-date, Boeing has delivered 143 airplanes valued at $10.2 billion compared to 130 deliveries valued at $9.1 billion a year ago. The increase is driven by the Boeing 737 MAX program, which saw deliveries increase by 9 units, while the Boeing 777 and Boeing 767 deliveries increased by one unit for each program, and Boeing 787 deliveries increased by 2 units. While the increase in Boeing 737 MAX deliveries is somewhat soft, this merely seems to be a timing issue and not a major reason for concern.

The book-to-bill ratio, an indicator of supply-demand health, was 0.7x in terms of orders and 0.8x in terms of value. For the first quarter, these ratios were 1.31x and 1.4x, reflecting continued healthy order inflow with upward pressure on production from a demand perspective.

Conclusion: Boeing's March Performance Does Not Overshadow Full-Year Growth

Boeing’s order inflow dropped year-on-year, but that is not driven by any lack of demand. Occasionally, we see bigger airplane orders that do not happen every year, and in March 2025, that was the case with the order for Korean Air, while this year did not sport an order of similar size. On deliveries, we note that March numbers were somewhat soft and did not show the typical quarter-end surge. This was driven by correction of the wiring issue on the Boeing 737 MAX but is not expected to have an impact on the full-year delivery numbers. As a result, I believe that Boeing is continuing to improve its framework to increase production, and we note that slowly but surely we will see elements coming together to produce the Boeing 737 MAX at even higher rates with the fourth line in operations in preparation for the certification of the MAX 10 later this year. It is a slow process with some setbacks, but Boeing is pacing things exactly the way it should, and that is with safety and quality as the cornerstone.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Logitech: Not Enough Growth To Pull Me In
data.description:
Introduction

With the recent correction in Logitech’s (LOGI) share price, I wanted to take a look at the company’s performance in 2025 to see if it would be a good time to start a position. It seems cheap on paper; however, the growth is not very exciting. Even though I am a fan of its products, the current valuation is not appealing.

The Performance

In general, LOGI’s 2025 top-line performance was fine. It wasn’t anything special because, at the end of 2025, its top line grew about 5.9%. The flat Q1 performance in 2025 pushed it down from around 6%+, which was also not great. The consistent positive growth did translate into a smooth upward trajectory overall.

If we look into the revenue segments in more detail, we can see which ones performed better than others. Here, we can see that Gaming is still the largest revenue contributor, but it is not growing particularly impressively. The standouts are clearly the Tablet Accessories and Pointing Devices, each growing 20.8% and 11.1%, respectively.

Gaming, for some reason, was noted as a growth engine, but I don’t see it. The management said that gaming sales were near pandemic-high levels, which benefited from innovation that was launched just ahead of the holiday season. In terms of regions, the Americas continue to disappoint, not just for Logitech but for many companies I covered in the past. EMEA saw growth of around 7%, and Asia Pacific grew a respectable 14% on the back of strong sales from China.

Looking at the company’s profitability over the last year, we can see that things began to improve from mid-25, with most of the margins higher, except for slight declines in gross margins. The graph may look like it is a big decline; however, it’s only 24 bps. It seems that the company managed to maintain its cost structure, and its operating leverage allowed it to improve profitability overall.

The company’s premium portfolios, like MX and Pro Gaming, most likely helped the company’s overall performance. Premium workspace products played a massive role here, too.

Now, looking at other key efficiency and profitability metrics, I was surprised at how high the company’s ROA, ROE, and ROTC are, especially for a hardware company. These sorts of returns are common with the best-run companies out there, and I am quite impressed. All of the metrics below are at least double what I look for as a minimum. The management is doing a commendable job at utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder value efficiently, which should be creating value in the long run. It also seems that the company has decent pricing power, which I wouldn’t have guessed, seeing that it competes in a highly competitive, and I would say, commoditized consumer hardware market.

Let’s dig into the company’s financial position. Logitech finished the year with around $1.8B in cash and equivalents, against zero in long-term debt. That is a very strong financial position off the bat. Add on top of this its ability to generate over $834m in operating cash flow for the last 9 months; the company can put all of that cash-generating capital to further the growth of the company through more innovation and experimentation.

Overall, it seems like the company is operating as a mature business, with very little growth happening over the last while, and I don’t know if that is going to change going forward. The company has been around for over 45 years at this point, so it is not a growth story any longer. I can see why it is so easy to generate such high returns. Everything is already in place, so the costs are not as high because there is hardly a need to have reinvestment needs at this point.

Upcoming Quarter

Logitech is going to report its Q4 numbers on the 5th of May, so it’s worth going through what we should expect. The management said sales should grow at around 3%-5%, while the gross margin should be between 43% and 44%. Non-GAAP operating income in the range of $155m-$165m, which translates to around 20% y/y growth. So, nothing impressive once again, apart from much higher profitability, since 20% growth is quite impressive.

Can the Company Still Grow at a Much More Attractive Pace?

The growth in sales over the last few years has not been particularly impressive, to say the least. Ever since the massive boost it got from the pandemic era, its operations have begun to look quite lackluster.

The management says that they see good momentum in B2B, which would be good if that were the case. B2C has always been less than predictable. The company’s push into enterprise revenues is a welcome move because it opens up more predictable revenue streams that rely mostly on hardware updates every couple of years. Enterprise is where all the money is, anyway, so I’d like to see a lot more initiative taken to get this off the ground as quickly as possible.

Further expansion globally is also a good way to get higher revenue growth in the future. APAC seems to be the promising growth driver going forward, especially now that the Americas are experiencing some softness, and it’s hard to know when this softness is going to improve. The question is whether Logitech can keep pushing its product mix towards more premium products and grow at a faster pace than the overall market. Their products are great. I’ve owned their MX Mini wireless keyboard for years now, and I have no complaints. The quality was much higher than I expected, and I would even say the price was not as high as the product feels. I think they have a decent chance of continuing to churn out great-quality products, especially in the office setting where the person needs to sit at a computer for hours on end, so the product needs to feel very comfortable and blend in without taking away from the overall efficiency and experience of the work environment.

Valuation

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model to get an idea of what the company is worth. I’m going to keep it relatively simple. It is a mature company, as we know, so I’m not expecting miracles in growth, only a consistent improvement as it has achieved in the past. Below are the assumptions I used.

Revenue growth is going to be 4%. I don’t see any reason to grow it at a higher pace than that, at least for now. For margins, I went with a slight improvement, as I expect the product mix to continue to shift towards more premium offerings. The rest I went with the company’s historical averages, for simplicity. So, with these assumptions, we get the following projections.

I think the assumptions are quite decent and achievable and portray a company that just keeps chugging along, minding its own business. With that said, these projections tell us that the company’s intrinsic value is around $103.5 a share, which means Logitech is trading close to its fair price.

Closing Comments

So, based on the company’s lackluster performance, even if it is heading in the right direction, I don’t see the appeal of owning it at these prices. There is just not enough growth for me to get excited about. The softness in Gaming and the Americas really put a damper on its performance. I think the assumptions are quite spot on, so if it doesn’t grow better than what I project, then there is no point in starting a position, as there may be better plays out there.

data.title: Micron: This Party Isn't Ending Anytime Soon
data.description:
Back in January, I reiterated my Strong Buy rating on Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) and expressed my view that the party was just getting started. I cited extraordinarily robust fiscal Q1 results, impressive guidance, and a valuation that was still at a major discount. Recently, after a dip, the stock has come storming back, and as of this writing, Micron is ~15% above levels when my previous article came out. The memory chip giant reported their latest results just last month, and today we'll take a look at those results as well as go through some recent developments that may have implications for their outlook.

Below, it is shown that Micron is firing on all cylinders. Demand continued to outstrip supply in Q2, and margins are seeing unbelievable amounts of expansion. The outlook seems strong at this point, with Q3 guidance being robust and production capacity set to expand moving forward but recently there have been efforts by a hyperscaler to reduce memory needed to run LLMs. There are some risks that demand will soften a bit, but with the valuation being at extremely depressed levels, the risk/reward remains highly opportunistic at this stage. As a result, I'm again reiterating my Strong Buy rating on Micron.

Growth Skyrockets

Let's start today with a look at their top-line numbers. For FY2026 Q2, Micron reported revenues of $23.86 billion, up 196.3% YoY. You can see in the above chart that growth has accelerated sharply from the previous quarter and is easily at its highest level in recent memory. Revenues hit a new record and blew past analyst expectations by over $4 billion, and so I believe it is quite clear that demand has been exceptional. Furthermore, in their earnings presentation, they stated that "Both AI and traditional server demand are constrained by lack of adequate DRAM and NAND supply" and so if not for these constraints, the top line could have been even stronger. At this point, demand is outstripping supply, and that's not such a bad problem to have.

For Q2, Micron saw broad strength as both their DRAM and NAND saw robust revenue increases both YoY and QoQ. Therefore, while DRAM has taken much of the spotlight in recent times, both technologies are driving growth for the company as a whole. As you can see above, bit shipments actually aren't up that much from the previous quarter due to the supply constraints mentioned above. The significant driver for the Q2 strength is huge increases in ASPs. This clearly shows that at this point their pricing power is probably at unprecedented levels. This should bode well for their margins, which will be examined next.

Margin Expansion Near Unbelievable

While there's no question that PC makers and smartphone makers have felt the memory supply crunch headwinds, Micron is benefitting immensely. For the MCBU and AEBU segments that are mostly focused on consumer products, revenues soared and gross margins exploded.

In addition to that, gross margin also saw major improvements in their key CMBU and CDBU segments as well. Overall, a look at the gross margin improvement for Q2 shows just how much pricing power they have. Later in this article, we will go over some developing risks, but as of the end of Q2, it is clear that clients are struggling to secure memory supply.

In the above table, you can see for each of the segments, the YoY expansion in operating margin was actually even larger than the gross margin improvement. This is a promising sign for investors, as it shows that Micron isn't just relying on the strong memory prices for profitability improvement. They are making real material improvements to their corporate efficiency, and that's helping to drive the bottom-line results.

Speaking of the bottom line, Micron reported an adjusted EPS of $12.20 for Q2, up 682% YoY. This growth rate is off the charts, and they also beat estimates by $3.04, which is extraordinary. There's no question that analyst consensus was quite high heading into their Q2 report, and the fact that they surpassed top and bottom line expectations with such ease shows that their business is firing on all cylinders, to say the least.

Outlook

Micron provided Q3 top-line and bottom-line guidance that was well above expectations. For revenue, the midpoint implies a 260% increase YoY. That would be another sharp acceleration from already breathtaking levels in Q2. For gross margin, their expectation of around 81% would be a massive expansion from 39% in the year-ago quarter, and so unsurprisingly, they expect pricing power to continue being strong. As for operating expenses, their $1.4 billion estimate represents just a 24% increase, and so investors can expect further operating margin strength as well moving forward. Lastly, the EPS midpoint projection of $19.15 is simply jaw-dropping, as they are hinting at over 900% of growth. In my view, this Q3 guidance is a mic drop moment for Micron, as I'm running out of vocabulary to describe their numbers.

Looking beyond Q3, the company also provided some sense of where the outlook is at. In their earnings call, CEO Sanjay Mehrotra stated that "we expect both DRAM and NAND industry bit demand in calendar 2026 to be constrained by supply" and that conditions will "remain tight beyond calendar 2026." If this materializes, Micron is obviously in a position to see continued tailwinds.

Supply Efforts

We will take a look at some risks in the next section, but at this point, Micron seems to be going all-in in efforts to boost supply. It is intuitive that with demand being so strong, they're trying to expand their production capacity. Citing an "unprecedented gap between supply and demand" the company's expansion plans are progressing aggressively. For DRAM, they have broken ground at their New York site and are starting ground preparation for their second Idaho fab. For NAND, they decided to break ground at their Singapore site, with initial wafer output expected in H2 CY2028.

Such ambitious supply efforts require a lot of capital, and their CapEx expectations clearly reflect this. For FY2026, CapEx is now expected to be above $25 billion, and for FY2027, they are projecting another meaningful step up. Given that adjusted FCF for Micron increased by over 700% YoY for Q2 to $6.899 billion, the company will likely be able to sustain this kind of capital spending, but will these investments pay off as expected? Let's talk about this next.

Risks Could Be Emerging

In late March, Alphabet's Google (GOOGL) introduced TurboQuant, Quantized Johnson-Lindenstrauss, and PolarQuant. These algorithms were released in a notable effort to run large language models and vector search engines with less memory. Of course this is a potential threat to Micron, as it could reduce the unrelenting demand that we have been seeing. Their pricing power could be at risk. Just a day later, as written by Seeking Alpha's Brandon Evans, Morgan Stanley (MS) analysts expressed that:

Google's TurboQuant, a compression algorithm that optimally addresses the challenge of memory overhead in vector quantization, will likely lead to the usage of more intensive AI applications rather than diluting demand for memory and storage products.

Morgan Stanley reiterated their overweight rating on Micron, but in my view, we do not know the full extent of the effects these new algorithms may have. That said, on March 31st, it was reported that mainstream DDR5 16 GB DRAM prices have pulled back by 6% since Micron's Q2 earnings report, and Citi (C) cut their price target from $510 down to $425 as a result. We may be seeing a beginning of a softening in demand that wasn't expected. Earlier this month, GF Securities came out and expressed that this spot memory price decline is likely nothing more than just a correction. After this flurry of news, I believe that it's too early to panic, but at the same time, investors should definitely keep their eyes peeled for further signs that demand is softening.

Valuation Provides Huge Margin For Error

Micron's forward P/E ratio is currently at a dirt-cheap level of just 7.94. While this is obviously due to high earnings expectations moving forward, I would say that such a depressed reading shows that the risk/reward remains at very attractive levels for investors.

Today, it was shown that Micron is firing on all cylinders for a wide spectrum of metrics. Top-line growth accelerated sharply, margins expanded impressively, and the bottom line is seeing growth that's basically in uncharted territory. Guidance for Q3 is very robust as well. The company is justifiably aggressively working to expand their production capacity, but as discussed above, some risks may be emerging. Very reputable firms like Morgan Stanley have come to their defense, but at this point, investors should at least be aware that there have been efforts to reduce the amount of memory needed.

Despite some risks, with the valuation at rock-bottom levels, I would say that the margin for error is comfortable right now. As of this writing, Seeking Alpha shows that their non-GAAP forward P/E is at a 65.83% discount to the IT sector while they're one of the most important semiconductor companies in the world. I believe this shows Micron stock remains a very attractive investment.

Concluding Remarks

As stated at the beginning, I expressed my view that the party was just getting started for Micron in my previous article. After today's analysis, I believe it would be fair to conclude that this party isn't ending anytime soon. After just moderate gains in the stock after my January update, Micron seems in a position to deliver outperformance moving forward, with the fundamentals and outlook being exceptional and the valuation leaving a good margin for error. Therefore, I have decided to reiterate my Strong Buy rating on the stock for a second straight time.

data.title: PG: Earnings Next Week Can Spur A Rally
data.description:
Procter & Gamble (PG) stock has had a wild ride to start the year. After melting up with the risk shift into defensives where the XLP was bid, the stock and the consumer staples sector broadly have come under pressure in recent weeks. The company is due to report earnings on April 24, and with the CAGNY update having provided confirmation that things have slowed, with 1H FY '26 growth at just 1%, the company needs to deliver a solid fiscal third quarter in order to get investors excited here.

Business Analysis

PG is a heavily diversified HPC giant across skin & personal care, hair care, grooming, baby and family care. The portfolio has started to see weakness in the fabric care and family care subsegments, while baby and feminine care can't seem to get off the ground after a flat first half. Fabric and Family Care were both negative in 1H of FY26, down 1% and 5%, respectively.

For context, Fabric and Home Care are approximately 35% of the business, with Baby, Feminine and Family Care comprising 25%. The second-largest division cannot be under pressure to this extent, or results will simply suffer. Beauty makes up 18%, Health Care makes up 14%, and Grooming is the balance at around 8%.

Source: Investor Presentation

To better understand why this is happening and to examine how this might change going forward, let's see what Q2 unpacked in this segment. For the combined Baby, Feminine and Family Care segment, pricing and mix were flat, while organic volume was down nearly 5%. While the company gained share in Baby Care, it lost share in feminine care and family care as private label continues to increase its foothold in the space. The company had category mix issues as well as higher tariff costs being passed through that are making private label products only more attractive versus the branded products from PG in a time of financial stress for the consumer. It's also worth noting, however, that the 9% earnings decline that this segment contributed to the total enterprise was exacerbated by a proactive marketing investment, which should pay forward dividends for the company in Q3 and Q4.

Let's revisit the guide ahead of earnings:

  • Sales +1-5%
    • FX and M&A (Inorganic) +1%
  • Organic sales growth 0%-4%
  • Diluted EPS +1-6%
  • Core EPS growth 0-4% to $6.83-$7.09

The guide is more involved than this with more specific details around commodity costs, tariffs, and interest and tax, but for discussion purposes, I simply want to focus on the forward earnings trajectory as it will ultimately drive the stock. The track record of this company for the last five years has been about 7.5% compounded annually, yet the stock has put up a five-year return of just 5% ex-dividends, which is rather remarkable, all things considered. The appetite for stocks in the consumer staples sector has been minimal, and a lot of that has to do with organic sales growth not being consistent nor robust.

The company last quarter cut their diluted EPS guide from 3-9% to the current 1-6% and going any lower than this would effectively mean they have to move to a lower end on the range that is negative - an actual earnings decline for this company.

While nothing is guaranteed, it's pretty clear that they will beat street estimates. That confidence stems from their earnings track record, where quarter after quarter they deliver EPS beats, seen below.

Source: Passage Research

Financial Assessment

So, what's the issue with organic growth? Coming out of COVID, the company had four solid fiscal years of OSG% growth. FY20-FY23 averaged 6.5% OSG. While this was no doubt driven by stock-up and other pandemic-related behaviors, it was still a very comfortable result. Since then, OSG% has faltered. FY24 through 1HFY26 has averaged just 2.5%.

The company has not been active on the M&A front such that there could even be a boost from the inorganic side of the business, too, which could help to drive a step function higher in earnings power.

Source: Investor Presentation

I like the setup going into the upcoming report principally because of the organic sales growth compares. The first half of the year faced rather difficult compares. The company put up 0% total OSG% growth with -1% volume growth and consequentially no earnings growth because the compares were difficult.

The slide below shows a really good way to think about back half compares. Q2 was their most difficult compare at a 3% OSG% lap, for which they put up a 0% OSG% figure this past quarter. That follows Q1 doing a 4% 2Y stack. A blended 3.5% in 1H can carry through the back half as the compares ease. As long as trends stay steady, we're looking at 2%+ OSG growth into the back half on average. The guide is for 0-4% OSG% growth on the year, and their first half has been a square 2% at the midpoint of the guide.

Source: Investor Presentation

With their OSG% results set to improve just because of a 2Y stack continuation against easy compares, I think what happens with guidance is they'll narrow the range; they will likely take the high and low end off and move to a 1-3% OSG% guide, and with an earnings beat this quarter, they can notch the guide back up, perhaps to the original level they cut from last quarter. This should be enough to spur a small rally in the shares.

I think it's important to remember that gross margins were under pressure because of grosses being down 50 bps as tariff impacts were not yet fully annualized and operating margins deleveraged significantly (70bps) on no organic sales growth. This should not be the case in Q3 with 2%+ OSG% growth.

Valuation and Trading Setup

The setup, in simple terms, is that the company needs to show back-half acceleration in organic sales growth in order to prompt solid equity performance here. I wouldn't be looking to this stock for 30% and 40% type of returns given its defensive nature, but this name could work for its earnings print if it puts one foot in front of the other on the OSG front. Getting back towards double-digit EPS growth would be another big milestone this year.

At the midpoint of the guide, we're at $6.96 in core EPS. If this can be held onto, then the stock is trading just shy of 21x. To put that in context, this is essentially one of the lowest valuations PG has received in the last 5 years. PG simply doesn't have the drawdowns that other stocks do. It's not a fair comparison to view PG relative to something like Exxon Mobil (XOM) or Tesla (TSLA), but for a defensive asset that will continue to grow earnings potential that is well off recent highs at a multi-year low valuation, I do like the setup here.

My preferred way to look at it is on an EV/EBITDA basis, as it places more emphasis on operating profitability for the business relative to the capital structure. PG is still hanging near the lows. At a low-teens multiple versus its own history of getting a high-teens EV/EBITDA multiple, stock does look like a relative bargain. The sector has de-rated, but this is one of the best-in-class companies in the space, and opportunities to pick up shares cheaply do not come around so often.

Source: Passage Research

Also, investors can get paid to be patient at this level. The dividend yield is effectively 3%. This is back towards the recent highs for the yield and tends to be an attractive point to invest in for the stock. That's well supported by a free cash flow yield that is quickly approaching 5%, a relatively unheard-of level for this stock.

Source: Passage Research

With the consumer staples sector having broken down in the last few weeks as there's been a large risk-on tilt in the market given the Iran war tension diffusion, the stock has come back down to earth after a vicious rally. Sitting below both of its core moving averages and having relatively neutral momentum on the RSI, now is the time when fundamentals can take over and drive the stock.

Source: Passage Research

Risks

Despite its defensive nature, investing in PG is not without risk. The company has experienced many recent headwinds from things like tariffs and higher operating costs, and the volume declines at the point of sale have not been there to offset the higher fixed expenses. Earnings power could be blunted further by rising opex. Management has also not been active on the inorganic front, so there are few kickers in the earnings power build to drive a recovery in growth. While 7 out of 10 product categories grew or held organic sales in fiscal Q2, their most recently reported quarter, their largest divisions are negative, forcing organic growth lower. Volume is currently negative, and if it remains there, it's difficult to paint a case where the stock recovers. More broadly, continued risk-on momentum for major indexes will put consumer staples only further in rotation mode, which may put pressure on the multiple.

Conclusion

PG seems to be well-positioned right now to beat earnings at the upcoming earnings report next week, largely driven by a beatable guide and easy compares. With a 3% dividend yield, an attractive free cash flow yield and an increasingly attractive valuation on both a P/E and EV/EBITDA basis, PG could start to work here. Easy compares should help to maintain the earnings guide and narrow the OSG% guide and provide a start to a momentum reversal higher in this name.
